Esther
Esther
Esther

Esther
Introduction

This account tells about the life of a young Jewish woman named Esther. These events happened back when King Ahasuerus (more widely known as King Xerxes) ruled over the kingdom of Persia. After Queen Vashti was banished for refusing to dance in front the king's guests at a drinking party, Esther was chosen as the new queen without revealing her Jewish background. But there was a high official called Haman who was offended by Esther's Jewish guardian and in his anger, determined to annihilate all of the Jews from the kingdom. This account reveals how Ether's courage and her love for her Jewish people leads her to help them and eventually helps to save their lives.
The account also reveals the source of a Jewish feast named âPurimâ, which to this day is a time when Jewish people celebrate how God saved them from their enemy, Haman.
Main components of this account
Esther becomes Queen of Persia
Haman's threat to the Jews
Haman's execution
The Jewish people destroy their enemies
This is still a very early look into the unfinished text of the Open English Translation of the Bible. Please double-check the text in advance before using in public.
Queen Vashti refuses King Ahasuerus
/s1 King Ahasuerus's banquet; Queen Vashti Deposed; Queen Vashti Defies King Xerxes; King Ahasuerus Deposes Queen Vashti; The King's Banquet
The following events happened during the reignÂŠ228506 of
King
AhasuerusÂŠ228503.
Ezr 4:6.
Also known as King Xerxes,
he ruled
the empire of Persia
withÂŠ228502 127 provincesÂŠ228514 from IndiaÂŠ228507 all the way across to EthiopiaÂŠ228510.)
In thoseÂŠ228517 daysÂŠ228516, he ruled his empire from
a city called
ShushanÂŠ228526,
Known as âSusaâ in many 20th century translations, even though earlier English translations often got it correct.
Persia's
capital.
In the third year of his reignÂŠ228531, he hosted a celebration for all of his officials and for every important person who worked for him, as well as the officers who served in the army of the
combined empire
of PersiaÂŠ228540 and MediaÂŠ228541, the wealthy landowners, and the officials of the provincesÂŠ228544. The king was presentÂŠ228545 in person
to host the celebration
which, over a period of six months, would displayÂŠ228547 the incredible wealth and prestige of his empire and his personal wealth and power.
ThenÂŠ228564 atÂŠ228564 the end of that time, the kingÂŠ228569 hosted
another

celebration, butÂŠ228564 this time for all the peopleÂŠ228572 in the royal fort in ShushanÂŠ228574, from the nobles to the least prestigious. He held this celebration over an entire week in the enclosed gardenÂŠ228584 of the royal palaceÂŠ228585.

There were white and blue curtains hanging from white and purpleÂŠ228596 cordsÂŠ228593 attached to silver rings on marble pillarsÂŠ228601. The guests reclined on goldÂŠ228605 and silver couchesÂŠ228603 set on a mosaic floor that was made of red and white marble, and mother-of-pearlÂŠ228612 and other expensive stonesÂŠ228613.
Drinks were servedÂŠ228615 inÂŠ228616 goldenÂŠ228617 goblets, andÂŠ228621 each one was a unique design. The king'sÂŠ228625 wine seemed limitless as he displayed his generosity,
andÂŠ228646 it was served accordingÂŠ228628 to his rule: ThereÂŠ228631 is no compulsion to drinkÂŠ228627, butÂŠ228627 all of his servants were to give each guest however much they wanted.
Meanwhile, Queen VashtiÂŠ228649 was hosting a celebration for the womenÂŠ228653
in a different part
of the king's royalÂŠ228655 palace.
/s1 Queen Vashti Deposed

On the seventh dayÂŠ228661 of the celebrations, when the king was feeling cheery from his wine-drinking, he ordered seven of his chief servants (MehumanÂŠ228669, BizthaÂŠ228670, HarbonaÂŠ228671, BigthaÂŠ228672, AbagthaÂŠ228673, ZetharÂŠ228674, and Karkas)

to bringÂŠ228685 Queen VashtiÂŠ228688, wearing her royalÂŠ228693 crownÂŠ228692, to him inÂŠ228692 orderÂŠ228694 to show her beautyÂŠ228699 to the peopleÂŠ228695 and the officialsÂŠ228696, because sheÂŠ228704 was very attractive.
ButÂŠ228706 when the servants came and told her the king's commandÂŠ228710, Queen VashtiÂŠ228708 refusedÂŠ228706 to come. WhenÂŠ228706 the king found this out, he got very angryÂŠ228715 and
sat there
raging.
SoÂŠ228724 he
called over
his advisors who knew the law and could make good decisions, and discussed with them what he should do.
(His closestÂŠ228739 advisors were Karshena, ShetharÂŠ228743, AdmathaÂŠ228744, TarshishÂŠ228745, MeresÂŠ228746, MarsenaÂŠ228747, and Memukan. These sevenÂŠ228749 officials were from
various places in the empire
of PersiaÂŠ228752 and MediaÂŠ228753 and they advised the kingÂŠ228756 personally,
thus making them
the most powerful officials in the empire.)
âAccordingÂŠ228761 to
our
law,â
the kingÂŠ228776 asked,
âwhat should be done withÂŠ228765 QueenÂŠ228773 VashtiÂŠ228766, since sheÂŠ228772 refused to obey my commandÂŠ228775 when the servants passed it onÂŠ228765 to her?â
Then Memukan answered the king,
speaking loudly enough that

both the king and his officials could hear. âQueen VashtiÂŠ228794 has done wrong,â he said, âand not only against the king. She has also done wrong against all the officials and peopleÂŠ228806 groups in all the provincesÂŠ228810 that KingÂŠ228786 AhasuerusÂŠ228812
rules
Soon

women all over the empire will hear about what the queenÂŠ228819 did and they'll say, âKingÂŠ228829 AhasuerusÂŠ228830 commanded hisÂŠ228837 servants to bringÂŠ228832 Queen VashtiÂŠ228835 to him, butÂŠ228838 she didn'tÂŠ228838 go! Then women will stop respecting theirÂŠ228826 husbandsÂŠ228826.
Even today, the leading women of PersiaÂŠ228847 and MediaÂŠ228849 will hear what the queenÂŠ228855 did and start doing the same to
their husbands who are
officialsÂŠ228857, and this will cause
a wave of
contemptÂŠ228860 and angerÂŠ228861.
If it pleases you
to do so
your majesty

, you should personally issue a royal decree and have it added to the laws of PersiaÂŠ228876 and MediaÂŠ228878 which cannot be repealed. This decree should say that VashtiÂŠ228885 can never again comeÂŠ228884 into your presence, and then you should choose a different womanÂŠ228892 for the royal positionÂŠ228889âsomeone who's betterÂŠ228893 than herÂŠ228889.

YourÂŠ228905 decreeÂŠ228897 will be proclaimedÂŠ228896 right through your empire, even though it's so hugeÂŠ228906, andÂŠ228896 thenÂŠ228896 all womenÂŠ228910, irrespective of their status or position, will honourÂŠ228912 their husbandsÂŠ228913.
This seemed like a good idea to the king andÂŠ228919 his officialsÂŠ228923, so KingÂŠ228922 Ahasuerus followed the adviceÂŠ228920 of Memukan

and sent letters to every province in his empire. He wrote to every province using its own alphabet and to each peopleÂŠ228944 group in its own language. The letters said that men should be the masters over their wives and children, and that a husband should be able to give orders to his wife in his own native language
that she should understand and obey
Beautiful Esther enters the king's harem
/s1 Esther becomes queen; Esther Becomes Queen; Esther Made Queen
Some time later when KingÂŠ228963 AhasuerusÂŠ228964 no longer felt so angry, he
started to miss
VashtiÂŠ228968. ButÂŠ228969 when she'd disobeyed his command, he had made a decree
thatÂŠ228970 she could never come into his presence againÂŠ228977
SoÂŠ228979 some of the young men who served the king suggested to him, â
Your majesty, you shouldÂŠ228984 get a new wife for yourself.
You could tell your servants to look for young virginsÂŠ228987 who are very beautiful.

You could assign officers in each provinceÂŠ228996 of your empire to bring everyÂŠ229001 virgin who is very beautiful here to your capital city of ShushanÂŠ229010. They could stay in the harem for virgins under the custody of HegaiÂŠ229019, the castrated royal guardian who takes care of the young women who live there andÂŠ228991 he could arrange for them to receive beauty treatmentsÂŠ229025.
ThenÂŠ229027 you could decide which youngÂŠ229027 womanÂŠ229027 you like best andÂŠ229027 thenÂŠ229027 make her queen instead of VashtiÂŠ229034.â
The king liked what they suggestedÂŠ229036, so he did it.

At that time, there was a Jewish man named Mordekai living inÂŠ229046 the capital city of ShushanÂŠ229046. (He was from the tribe of Benjamin: his father was Jair, his grandfather was ShimeiÂŠ229054, and his great-grandfather was KishÂŠ229057
who'd been taken away from Jerusalem and led to Babylon when KingÂŠ229071 NebuchadnezzerÂŠ229076 of Babylon had exiledÂŠ229066 JeconiahÂŠ229070, the king of Judah, along with many of his people.)
Now Mordekai was taking care of his cousin, who was an orphan. WhenÂŠ229080 her fatherÂŠ229093 and mother had died, Mordekai had adoptedÂŠ229104 her as his own daughterÂŠ229087. HerÂŠ229102
Hebrew
name was HadassahÂŠ229084, and her
Persian
name was Esther. Esther was now a youngÂŠ229095 womanÂŠ229095 and she was exceptionally attractive.

SoÂŠ229109 when the king's command and decree became known, many youngÂŠ229116 women were being brought into the capital city of ShushanÂŠ229120 and placed under Hegai's charge, and this is what happened to EstherÂŠ229127. (Hegai was the overseer of the women.)

Hegai was very impressed withÂŠ229141 Esther, and he treated her favourablyÂŠ229143. He quicklyÂŠ229145 arranged for Esther to receive her beautyÂŠ229148 treatments and her allotment of food, as well as choosing sevenÂŠ229155 female servants from the king's palaceÂŠ229161 and assigningÂŠ229158 them to be her personal attendants. He also movedÂŠ229163 Esther and her attendants to the best rooms inÂŠ229141 the harem for virgins.
(EstherÂŠ229174 hadn't revealed her family ties or her Jewishness to anyone, because Mordekai had instructedÂŠ229183 her not to.)
Every day Mordekai would walkÂŠ229195 around inÂŠ229209 frontÂŠ229196 of the courtyardÂŠ229197 of the women'sÂŠ229200 harem to find out
from others
how EstherÂŠ229205 was doing andÂŠ229190 how she was being treated.
Each youngÂŠ229213 womanÂŠ229213 would get twelve months of beautyÂŠ229233 treatmentÂŠ229233 accordingÂŠ229224 to the
Persian
law for womenâthere would be six months of applying oilÂŠ229236 of myrrhÂŠ229237, thenÂŠ229211 six months of perfumesÂŠ229240 andÂŠ229211 other lotions
designed for
women. ThenÂŠ229211 theirÂŠ229233 turn would come, one atÂŠ229211 a timeÂŠ229232, to be taken to KingÂŠ229219 AhasuerusÂŠ229220.
TheÂŠ229250 youngÂŠ229245 womanÂŠ229245 was allowed to takeÂŠ229257 whatever she wanted from the haremÂŠ229259 when she went to the king'sÂŠ229264 houseÂŠ229263.

She'd be taken across in the eveningÂŠ229266, and in the morningÂŠ229270 she'd be led out to the other harem which was for the concubinesÂŠ229285. (That harem was overseen by ShaashgazÂŠ229281, another castrated royal guardian.) The womenÂŠ229276 taken there wouldn't go againÂŠ229289 to the king's houseÂŠ229275 unless he had especially liked them and summonedÂŠ229299 her back by nameÂŠ229300.
Esther becomes queen

Now Esther was the daughter of Mordekai's uncleÂŠ229309, AbihailÂŠ229308, from whom Mordekai had adopted her as his own daughter. WhenÂŠ229302 it was her turn to go to the king, she didn'tÂŠ229320 ask for anythingÂŠ229322 other than what HegaiÂŠ229329, the king's trusted official inÂŠ229302 charge of the harem, had advised, andÂŠ229302 everyoneÂŠ229333 who saw Esther liked her.
SoÂŠ229344 EstherÂŠ229345 was takenÂŠ229344 to KingÂŠ229348 AhasuerusÂŠ229349 atÂŠ229354 his royal palace in JanuaryÂŠ229359, in the seventhÂŠ229362 yearÂŠ229360 of his reignÂŠ229363,
and the kingÂŠ229366 lovedÂŠ229365 EstherÂŠ229369 more than any of the other women. He treated her more kindly and more favourablyÂŠ229375 than any of the other young women
who had become his concubines
. He put a royalÂŠ229384 crownÂŠ229382 onÂŠ229385 her headÂŠ229385 and made her the
new
queenÂŠ229386 to replace VashtiÂŠ229388.
ThenÂŠ229390 the kingÂŠ229391 hosted a large celebration and invited all his officialsÂŠ229396 and servantsÂŠ229397 to a feast to celebrate EstherÂŠ229400
becoming queen
. He proclaimed that this would be a time to celebrate for people in all of the provincesÂŠ229402 of his empire when they wouldn't have to pay taxes, and he gaveÂŠ229404
many
generous gifts.
Mordekai and Esther earn the king's favour
/s1 Mordecai Discovers a Plot; Mordecai and Haman; Mordecai Saves the King's Life; Mordecai Uncovers a Conspiracy
Later onÂŠ229409,
KingÂŠ229416 Ahasuerus had
his officers
bring more virginsÂŠ229410
to Shushan
. During this time, Mordekai
had been given a position by the king so
he sat
there
atÂŠ229409 the king'sÂŠ229416 gateÂŠ229414.
EstherÂŠ229419 had still not toldÂŠ229426 anyone that
she was Jewish
because Mordekai had warned herÂŠ229421 not to tell anyone.
What's more,
she continued to follow all of Mordekai's instructionsÂŠ229426, just as she had done when she was still under his guardianshipÂŠ229437.
/s1 Mordekai's Loyalty to the King
One day when Mordekai was
onÂŠ229445 duty
atÂŠ229445 the king's gateÂŠ229445, two of the king's guardsÂŠ229455 who protected the doorway
to the king's private rooms
became angryÂŠ229448 withÂŠ229445 the king and so they planned to assassinate KingÂŠ229447 AhasuerusÂŠ229461. (Their names were BigthanÂŠ229449 and TereshÂŠ229450.)
ButÂŠ229463 when Mordekai heard what they were planning, he told Queen EstherÂŠ229467 about it andÂŠ229469 she told the kingÂŠ229471, explaining that Mordekai had given her the information.
WhenÂŠ229475 the matterÂŠ229485 was investigatedÂŠ229475 and
the facts
discovered, both conspirators were impaled onÂŠ229484
sharpened wooden
poles, and in the presence of the kingÂŠ229488, the incident was recorded in the royal record of events.
Haman plans to annihilate the Jews
/s1 Haman's Plot against the Jews; Haman Plots to Destroy the Jews; Haman Undertakes to Destroy the Jews; Haman's Plot to Destroy the Jews
Some time later, KingÂŠ229496 AhasuerusÂŠ229497 promoted Haman, one of his officials (and the son of HammedathaÂŠ229503
who was a descendant
of Agag). The kingÂŠ229496 gave Haman a very important positionÂŠ229509âmore important than any of his other officials.

ThenÂŠ229517 the king commandedÂŠ229531 that all his other officials had to bow down inÂŠ229523 front of Haman to honour him, andÂŠ229517 they were doing this atÂŠ229523 the king's gateÂŠ229523, except for Mordekai who refused to bow down to reverence Haman.
âWhyÂŠ229552 are youÂŠ229550 disobeying the king's commandÂŠ229553?â asked the other royal servantsÂŠ229542 atÂŠ229546 the king's gateÂŠ229546.
Mordekai told them that it was because he was a Jew
(andÂŠ229556 Jews only worship Yahweh)
. He didn'tÂŠ229562 listenÂŠ229563 to their
warnings
so they told HamanÂŠ229566 about it to seeÂŠ229567 if he would tolerateÂŠ229568 Mordekai inÂŠ229557 keeping refusing to bow down.
WhenÂŠ229580 Haman sawÂŠ229580 that Mordekai would not bow down to him or show him reverence, heÂŠ229589 was furious.
ButÂŠ229593 Haman despisedÂŠ229593 the idea of just punishing Mordekai because he'd been told that Mordekai
didn't bow to him because
Mordekai was a Jew. SoÂŠ229593 HamanÂŠ229609 decided to annihilateÂŠ229610 all of Mordekai's people, i.e., all the Jews throughout the kingdomÂŠ229619 of AhasuerusÂŠ229620.
In early April of the twelfth year of KingÂŠ229633 Ahasuerus' reign, HamanÂŠ229640 got his servants to throwÂŠ229635 dice
to select the best month and the best day to kill the Jews
, and the 13th of March was chosen.
Then HamanÂŠ229656
went to
KingÂŠ229657 AhasuerusÂŠ229658 and said, â
Your Majesty,

thereÂŠ229659 is a certain group of people who live amongÂŠ229665 the other peoples in every provinceÂŠ229668 of your empire. They have theirÂŠ229670 own set of laws, so they don'tÂŠ229682 obey your laws. It's not good for you to allow them to continue to live in your empire.
Your majesty, if you approve of this plan, then write a decree saying that all of the Jews mustÂŠ229700 be destroyedÂŠ229694.
When they're dead, we can take all of theirÂŠ229694 possessions, andÂŠ229695 from that
I will give 300 tons of silverÂŠ229699 to your administrators for them to put into your royal treasuriesÂŠ229709.â
This seemed sensible to
the king so he removedÂŠ229712 his signet ring from his finger andÂŠ229712 he gaveÂŠ229719 it
Having the king's signet ring enabled Haman to stamp the king's seal (like a signature) onto the newly drafted laws.
to Haman (the son of HammedathaÂŠ229723, the AgagiteÂŠ229724, the adversary of the Jews).
The kingÂŠ229729 told HamanÂŠ229730, âYou can keep the moneyÂŠ229731 for yourself, andÂŠ229728 you can do whatever you want to do withÂŠ229738 those peopleÂŠ229734.â

On the thirteenth dayÂŠ229747 of that same monthÂŠ229743, Haman called in the royal scribes, and he dictated a letter to them. He toldÂŠ229754 them to send copies to the royal officialsÂŠ229771, the governorsÂŠ229762 of each province, and the leaders of each people group
within the empire

. The scribes translated the letter so it could be sent to each province using its own alphabet and to each people group in its own languageÂŠ229779. To show that he was sending the letter under the king's own authority, Haman sealedÂŠ229784 each copy of the letter withÂŠ229743 the ring that had the king's official seal onÂŠ229743 it.

Couriers delivered the letters to the officials in every provinceÂŠ229796 in the empire. The letters said to completely destroyÂŠ229798 all the Jews, includingÂŠ229800 the children andÂŠ229788 womenÂŠ229811, onÂŠ229790 a single day. That was to be the thirteenth day of March early in the following year. The letters also stated that those who killed the Jews could take everything that belonged to them.

The letter told the officials to displayÂŠ229835 copies where everyone could see themâthat way all the peopleÂŠ229838 in every single provinceÂŠ229833 would know that the king had commanded this, andÂŠ229834 they could get readyÂŠ229840 for when the dayÂŠ229841 came.

So couriersÂŠ229844 hurriedÂŠ229846 off withÂŠ229847 the letters as the king had commandedÂŠ229847. A herald in the capital cityÂŠ229857 of Shushan also proclaimed what the letters said. Then the king and HamanÂŠ229854 relaxed and drank together, butÂŠ229849 there was
total
confusionÂŠ229859 in Shushan
about how all of this would work out
Mordekai asks the queen for help
/s1 Mordecai Asks for Esther's Help; Esther Agrees to Help the Jews; Esther plans to rescue the Jews; Mordecai Requests Esther's Help; Mordecai Persuades Esther to Help
When Mordekai found out about what had been done, he tore his clothesÂŠ229874 and dressed in sackclothÂŠ229876 and
threw
ashesÂŠ229877
over himself (as a sign of mourning or distress)
and wentÂŠ229878 out into the cityÂŠ229880 centre, wailing loudlyÂŠ229883 and bitterlyÂŠ229884.
ButÂŠ229886 no one was wearing mourning garb was allowed inside the king's gate, so when Mordekai reached the gate, he had to stay just outside it.

In every provinceÂŠ229904 of the empire, the letter that said to destroy the Jews was publicly displayed and when the Jews heard about itÂŠ229921, they were incredibly upset. They skipped meals and wailed loudly, and many of them also wore sackclothÂŠ229919 and threw ashesÂŠ229920 on themselves and lay on the ground.
WhenÂŠ229924
Queen
Esther'sÂŠ229929 young female attendants cameÂŠ229924 with her guardians andÂŠ229924 told her
what was going on outside
, even the queenÂŠ229934 became very scared. She sentÂŠ229936 Mordekai some good clothesÂŠ229938 to wear instead of the sackclothÂŠ229943, butÂŠ229924 he wouldn't acceptÂŠ229946 them.

The kingÂŠ229952 had assigned some of the royal guardians to Esther personally, so Esther calledÂŠ229948 for one of themâa man named HathakÂŠ229950. She toldÂŠ229956 him to go out and speak with Mordekai and find out what he was so distressed about.
SoÂŠ229970 HathakÂŠ229971 wentÂŠ229970 out to
talk to
MordekaiÂŠ229974 who was out in the plaza in frontÂŠ229980 of the king'sÂŠ229983 gateÂŠ229981.
Mordekai told him everything thatÂŠ229998 Haman was planning to do andÂŠ229985 even told him how much moneyÂŠ229997 Haman had said the king would get for his treasuriesÂŠ230004 if
the king commanded people
to killÂŠ230009 all the Jews.

Mordekai also gaveÂŠ230022 Hathak a copy of the letter that the announcers had read out loud inÂŠ230020 ShushanÂŠ230020, that said that people must kill all the Jews. He told Hathak to show the letter to EstherÂŠ230027 so that she would know exactly what it said and also told him to urge her to go to the kingÂŠ230035 personally and to beg him desperately to save her people
from destructionÂŠ230021
SoÂŠ230045 HathakÂŠ230046 returned to the queen andÂŠ230047 told her what Mordekai had said,
andÂŠ230056 EstherÂŠ230054 toldÂŠ230056 himÂŠ230056 to go back to Mordekai with this message:

âThere's a law about going to the king that applies to everyoneÂŠ230071 in the kingdom, both men and women. If anyoneÂŠ230089 goes into the innerÂŠ230084 courtyardÂŠ230083 of the palace, where the king can see them, and the king has not summoned them, that person will be executed. Only if the king holdsÂŠ230094 out his goldenÂŠ230101 scepterÂŠ230100 to them, then they will live. (EveryoneÂŠ230089 in the whole empire knows this law.) The king hasn't called for me in over a month
, and if I go in without being summoned, I could be put to deathÂŠ230091
SoÂŠ230117 Hathak
went back to Mordekai andÂŠ230117 told him what the queen had said.
Mordekai told
Hathak
to tell this to EstherÂŠ230129: âDon't imagineÂŠ230132 that just because you live there inÂŠ230133 the king's palace that you'll be safe when they kill all the other Jews.

If you say nothing atÂŠ230147 all now, someone from some other placeÂŠ230153 will rescue the Jews, butÂŠ230150 you and your relatives will not survive. Who knows, perhaps it was for a time just like this that
God allowed
you to become queenÂŠ230167?â
ThenÂŠ230169 EstherÂŠ230170 sent this replyÂŠ230171 to MordekaiÂŠ230174:

âGo and assemble all the Jews who live here in ShushanÂŠ230184, and tell them to fast and pray for my sakeâtell them to not eat or drinkÂŠ230192 anything for three days and three nightsÂŠ230195. My female attendants and I will also fast in the same way. At the end of the three days, I will go in to talk to the king even though it's against the law. I will do thatÂŠ230208 even if it costs me my life.â
SoÂŠ230216 MordekaiÂŠ230217 wentÂŠ230216 andÂŠ230218 did everythingÂŠ230219 that EstherÂŠ230224 had toldÂŠ230222 him to do.
Esther goes to the king without an invitation
/s1 Esther's Banquet; Esther Invites the King and Haman to a Banquet; Esther's Request to the King
Three days later, the
Queen
EstherÂŠ230232 put on her royal robesÂŠ230231 and
went and

stoodÂŠ230234 in the innerÂŠ230239 courtyardÂŠ230235 of the palace, across from the king's house. He was in the royal palace, sittingÂŠ230244 on the royal throneÂŠ230247 and facing the room's entranceÂŠ230252.

As soon as the king noticedÂŠ230256 Esther standingÂŠ230262 there inÂŠ230263 the courtyardÂŠ230263, he was very pleased to see her. SoÂŠ230255 he held out his goldenÂŠ230273 scepter to her and Esther came up
to the throne
and touchedÂŠ230278 the top of the scepter.
âWhy have you come here, Queen EstherÂŠ230289?â the kingÂŠ230285 asked. âWhat do you want? Tell me, and I'll give you anything you ask forâeven half of the empire.â
âIf it pleases your majesty,â EstherÂŠ230302 replied, âplease comeÂŠ230309 with HamanÂŠ230311 today to the banquetÂŠ230315 thatÂŠ230316 I'veÂŠ230318 prepared for you.â
The king said to his servants, â
Go and

get Hamanâ the king told his servants, âand bringÂŠ230323 him quicklyÂŠ230323 so that we can do what the queen has asked us.â SoÂŠ230321 the king and Haman wentÂŠ230332 to the banquetÂŠ230337 that EstherÂŠ230331 had had prepared for them.
While they were drinkingÂŠ230346 wine, the kingÂŠ230344 saidÂŠ230343 to EstherÂŠ230345, âNow please tell me what you
really
want. I'll give you anything you ask for, no matter how much it is. I truly mean what I am saying.â
âThis is what I
really
want.â EstherÂŠ230363 replied.
âYour majesty, if you're pleased with me and if you're happy to give me what I want, please comeÂŠ230388 with HamanÂŠ230390 to
